Immediate danger state; need for healing items or defensive upgrades

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tartarus environment; dungeon aesthetics; descent into underworld

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Wooden club as starting weapon; basic shield; limited enemy variety in early areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Weaker attack power; lower damage output; slower combat progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Upgraded weapons with projectile abilities (Staff of Fennel fireball, Divine Sword lightning); special equipment acquisition
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased damage range; enhanced combat versatility; new area accessibility

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition to darker environments; Tartarus visual aesthetic; appearance of Hades' strongest minions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Intensified enemy encounters; two-stage final boss fight; mandatory confrontation with shadow form

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Liberation of Helene; underworld threat eliminated; restoration of peace
Darker color palette; underworld dungeon architecture; concentration of mythological creatures
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Maximum enemy density; highest difficulty encounters; final confrontation location

environmental_storytelling.example: Top-down map of ancient Greece showing eight distinct locations (Arcadia, Argolis, Attica, Crete, Laconia, Peloponnes, Phrygia, Phthia) guides player journey and establishes spatial narrative context
Specific Greek mythological enemies (Minotaur, Cyclops, Nemean Lion, etc.) serve as environmental indicators of danger zones and thematic consistency with classical Greek setting
Weapon progression narrative (wooden club → Staff of Fennel → Nymph's Sword → Divine Sword) visually demonstrates hero growth and divine intervention timeline
Named equipment referencing Greek gods (Harp of Apollo, Sword of Hephaestus, Staff of Prometheus, Sandals of Hermes) reinforces divine alliance narrative and mythological authenticity
Three love fragments serve as tangible collectibles representing abstract narrative motivation (Orpheus' affection for kidnapped Helene)
Visual shift from classical Greek city-state aesthetics to underworld dungeon design marks narrative descent and danger escalation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Limited offensive reach; basic melee attacks with wooden club; ground-bound movement
Projectile attack capability (fireball/lightning); enhanced mobility via Pegasus mount; increased combat range
Full arsenal deployment; maximum equipment; capable of two-stage boss battle engagement
Grant power-ups; provide equipment upgrades; offer optional side quests; non-hostile interaction
Hostile engagement; serve as progression gates; area-specific spawning; increasing difficulty with game progression
Blind fight format; shadow reflection combat; evasive/illusory tactics
Revealed true form; maximum threat level; ultimate underworld ruler manifestation
Blocks access to Tartarus; multi-headed threat; symbolic underworld sentinel

notes: The Battle of Olympus communicates world-state primarily through Greek mythological theming and environmental progression. The game uses a non-linear overworld map structure with eight distinct ancient Greek locations that serve as safe/exploration zones, while dungeons function as danger spaces. Visual manifestation of danger relies heavily on mythological creature design and enemy placement density. Faction communication is implicit—Olympian gods represent beneficial forces (through acquired equipment and special abilities), while Hades' underworld represents malevolent opposition. The narrative progression from early-game vulnerability to late-game empowerment is visualized through weapon upgrade sequences and special item acquisition. The two-stage final boss encounter (shadow vs. true form) represents psychological/metaphysical layers of threat. Love fragments provide emotional-narrative grounding within the mechanical progression system. Sources do not provide detailed color palette or specific sprite descriptions, limiting granular visual analysis.
